title: Optimistic vs Pessimistic Locking
body:
# Optimistic vs Pessimistic Locking

## Hook — a motivating scenario

Two admins open the same product to edit. They both load price = $10, both change it, both save —
and the second silently overwrites the first's change (a **lost update**). You can prevent it two
opposite ways: **lock the row** so the second admin waits (pessimistic), or **let both proceed** but
**reject the second save** because the data changed underneath it (optimistic). Which is right depends
entirely on **how often they actually collide**.

## Mental model — assume conflict (lock first) vs assume no conflict (check at the end)

The two strategies embody opposite bets about contention:
- **Pessimistic locking** assumes conflicts are **likely**, so it **locks first** (`SELECT … FOR
  UPDATE`): acquire the lock before reading/writing; others **wait**. Conflicts are prevented by
  exclusion.
- **Optimistic locking** assumes conflicts are **rare**, so it **doesn't lock**: read freely, and at
  write time **check that the data hasn't changed** (via a **version number** or timestamp); if it has,
  **reject and retry**. Conflicts are detected, not prevented.

```compare
{
  "options": [
    { "label": "Pessimistic (lock first)", "points": ["Acquire lock before working; others block/wait", "Prevents conflicts up front", "Best when contention is HIGH", "Risks: reduced concurrency, deadlocks, lock waits"] },
    { "label": "Optimistic (check at commit)", "points": ["No locks; verify version unchanged at write", "Detects conflicts; loser retries", "Best when contention is LOW", "Risks: wasted work + retries under high contention"] }
  ]
}
```

## Build it up — how optimistic locking works

Add a **version** column. Read it with the row; on update, include it in the WHERE clause and bump it:

```
UPDATE products SET price = 12, version = version + 1
WHERE id = 7 AND version = 5;   -- 0 rows affected ⇒ someone else changed it ⇒ conflict
```

If another transaction already moved version 5 → 6, your `WHERE version = 5` matches **0 rows**, so you
detect the conflict and retry (re-read, re-apply). This is the **atomic conditional update** from the
transactions chapter, and it's exactly how MVCC-based snapshot isolation surfaces write conflicts. No
locks are held while the user "thinks" — great when collisions are rare (the two-admins case usually
*is* rare).

```reveal
{
  "prompt": "How does a version column turn the 'two admins overwrite each other' lost update into a safe, detectable conflict?",
  "answer": "Both admins read the product including its version, say version = 5. Admin A saves first with `UPDATE ... SET price=11, version=6 WHERE id=7 AND version=5` — it matches the row (still v5), updates it, and bumps the version to 6. Admin B then saves with the same guard `WHERE id=7 AND version=5` — but the row is now v6, so B's WHERE matches 0 rows: the update affects nothing. B's application sees '0 rows affected,' recognizes the optimistic-lock conflict (the data changed since they read it), and handles it — typically re-reading the now-current value and either re-applying their change or prompting the user ('this was edited by someone else'). So instead of B silently clobbering A's write (lost update), the version check makes the conflict explicit and recoverable. The key is that the version comparison and the update happen atomically in one statement, so there's no read-then-write gap for a race to slip through. It converts a silent overwrite into a detected, retryable conflict — without holding any lock while the admins were editing."
}
```

## Build it up — choosing by conflict rate

The decision is driven by **how often transactions actually collide on the same data**:
- **Low contention → optimistic.** Most transactions don't conflict, so locking everyone would waste
  concurrency; the rare conflict is cheaply handled by a retry. (Typical of user-edits, most web CRUD.)
- **High contention → pessimistic.** If many transactions fight over the same hot rows, optimistic
  retries pile up — everyone keeps failing and re-doing work (a *retry storm*, wasted effort). Locking
  serializes them once and avoids the churn.

So: **optimistic shines when conflicts are rare** (no lock overhead, high concurrency); **pessimistic
shines when conflicts are common** (avoids endless retries). Pessimistic's cost is reduced concurrency
and **deadlocks** (recall transactions: acquire locks in a consistent order); optimistic's cost is
**wasted work + retries** under contention.

```reveal
{
  "prompt": "Why does optimistic locking become a poor choice under HIGH contention, and pessimistic become poor under LOW contention?",
  "answer": "Under high contention, many transactions touch the same hot rows, so with optimistic locking they all read the same version, all try to commit, and all but one fail the version check — then retry, read the new version, and likely collide again. This creates a retry storm: lots of repeated, wasted work and poor throughput, and some transactions may starve (keep losing). Pessimistic locking handles high contention better by serializing access once — the second transaction simply waits for the lock instead of doing-then-discarding work repeatedly. Conversely, under low contention, conflicts are rare, so pessimistic locking pays a constant cost for nothing: every transaction acquires (and others wait on) locks even though they'd almost never have collided, needlessly reducing concurrency and adding lock management and deadlock risk. Optimistic locking under low contention has near-zero overhead (no locks held during the think-time) and the rare conflict is cheaply retried. So the strategies are matched to the conflict rate: optimistic = cheap when collisions are rare but degrades into retry storms when they're frequent; pessimistic = safe under frequent collisions but wastes concurrency when they're rare. Measure or estimate your conflict rate to choose."
}
```

Slide along the contention axis to see which strategy wins as collisions get more frequent:

```tradeoff
{ "title": "How should you control concurrency as contention rises?", "axis": { "left": "Optimistic (no locks, detect-and-retry)", "right": "Pessimistic (lock first, others wait)" }, "steps": [ { "label": "Very low contention", "detail": "Transactions almost never collide. Optimistic wins: no locks held during think-time, near-zero overhead, high concurrency, and the rare conflict is cheaply handled by a version-check retry." }, { "label": "Moderate contention", "detail": "Occasional collisions on shared rows. Optimistic still works but retries start appearing; a hybrid (optimistic generally, pessimistic on known hot rows) keeps wasted work in check." }, { "label": "High contention", "detail": "Many transactions fight over hot rows. Optimistic degrades into retry storms — all but one fail the version check, redo work, and likely collide again, hurting throughput." }, { "label": "Very high contention", "detail": "Pessimistic wins: SELECT ... FOR UPDATE serializes contenders once so the second simply waits instead of doing-then-discarding work, at the cost of reduced concurrency and deadlock risk." } ] }
```

## In the wild

- **ORMs** (Hibernate, Rails, Django) support **optimistic locking** via a `version`/`lock_version`
  column — common for user-facing edits (low contention).
- **`SELECT … FOR UPDATE`** (pessimistic) is used for hot, high-contention rows (inventory decrement on
  a popular item, sequence allocation, financial rows).
- **MVCC snapshot isolation** (previous chapter) is essentially optimistic — conflicts detected at
  commit (serialization errors → retry).
- The choice maps to the **concurrency-control decision** introduced in the transactions chapter, now
  with the conflict-rate framing.

## Common misconception — "optimistic locking is the modern/better choice; always avoid locks"

It's better *only when contention is low*; under high contention it's worse than locking.

```reveal
{
  "prompt": "Why is 'always prefer optimistic (lock-free) concurrency' wrong advice?",
  "answer": "Because optimistic concurrency's advantage is conditional on low contention. It avoids holding locks and gives high concurrency precisely when transactions rarely collide — the common case for user edits and most CRUD, where it's an excellent default. But its mechanism is 'do the work, then check for a conflict at commit, and retry if you lost.' Under high contention on hot rows, that means lots of transactions repeatedly doing work and throwing it away as they fail the version check and retry — a retry storm with poor throughput and possible starvation. In that regime, pessimistic locking is better: it serializes contenders once (they wait rather than redo), avoiding wasted work. So 'always avoid locks' ignores that locks are the right tool when collisions are frequent. The correct framing is to match the strategy to the actual conflict rate: optimistic for low-contention (most cases), pessimistic for high-contention hot spots — and sometimes a hybrid (optimistic generally, pessimistic on known hot rows). Neither is universally 'modern' or 'better'; the conflict rate decides."
}
```

The choice is a **bet on contention**: **optimistic** (no locks; detect-and-retry) wins when conflicts
are **rare**; **pessimistic** (lock-first; others wait) wins when conflicts are **common**. Optimistic
isn't universally better — under high contention its **retries** are worse than locking.

## Self-test

```quiz
{
  "question": "Optimistic locking handles concurrency by:",
  "options": [
    "Locking the row before anyone can read it",
    "Letting transactions proceed without locks, then checking a version at write time and retrying if it changed",
    "Preventing all writes",
    "Using synchronized clocks"
  ],
  "answer": 1,
  "explanation": "Optimistic = no locks held; detect conflicts at commit via a version/timestamp check and retry the loser. Pessimistic locks up front."
}
```

```quiz
{
  "question": "You should prefer pessimistic locking when:",
  "options": [
    "Conflicts are rare",
    "Contention is high — many transactions fight over the same rows, so optimistic retries would storm",
    "You want maximum concurrency on rarely-contended data",
    "You never write data"
  ],
  "answer": 1,
  "explanation": "High contention makes optimistic retries pile up (wasted work); pessimistic serializes contenders once. Low contention favors optimistic."
}
```

## Recap — key terms

Flip each card to check yourself, then move through the deck:

```flashcards
{ "title": "Optimistic vs pessimistic locking — key terms", "cards": [ { "front": "Pessimistic locking", "back": "Assumes conflicts are likely, so it locks first (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE) before reading/writing; others wait. Conflicts are prevented by exclusion." }, { "front": "Optimistic locking", "back": "Assumes conflicts are rare, so it holds no locks: read freely, then check at write time that the data hasn't changed. Conflicts are detected, not prevented." }, { "front": "Lost update", "back": "Two transactions both read a value, both modify it, and the second save silently overwrites the first's change — the failure both locking strategies prevent." }, { "front": "Version column / atomic conditional update", "back": "Optimistic's mechanism: include the read version in the WHERE clause and bump it on update. If 0 rows are affected, someone else changed the row — a detected conflict." }, { "front": "Retry storm", "back": "Under high contention, optimistic transactions repeatedly fail the version check, redo work, and collide again — lots of wasted effort and poor throughput." }, { "front": "Choosing by conflict rate", "back": "Low contention favors optimistic (high concurrency, cheap retries); high contention favors pessimistic (serialize once, avoid retry storms)." } ] }
```

## Key takeaways

- **Pessimistic** locking **assumes conflicts** → lock first (`FOR UPDATE`), others wait; **optimistic**
  **assumes none** → no locks, **version-check at commit**, retry on conflict.
- Optimistic uses an **atomic conditional update** (version column); it's how **MVCC snapshot
  isolation** surfaces write conflicts.
- Choose by **conflict rate**: **low contention → optimistic** (high concurrency, cheap retries);
  **high contention → pessimistic** (avoid retry storms).
- Optimistic isn't universally better — its costs are **wasted work/retries** under contention;
  pessimistic's are **reduced concurrency + deadlocks**.

## Up next

That completes correctness & consensus. Next module: keeping replicas in sync and repairing
divergence. First up: **Quorums & Sloppy Quorums**.
